headline: After verifying the code live, the project's current rigor is low-to-moderate and not yet defensible to a skeptical examiner: a dumb letter-set-Jaccard baseline (no frequencies, no n-grams, no information theory) ties the trigram-JS headline EXACTLY at RF=0.455 — so the single highest-value fix is to add the alphabet-overlap and unigram-JS baselines (plus a within-language char-shuffle negative control) and either show trigrams beat them or honestly reframe the contribution, because right now nothing demonstrates the information theory is doing the work.

new_gaps.title: Trigram-JS is exactly tied by a dumb alphabet-overlap baseline (RF 0.455 = 0.455) — no evidence information theory adds anything
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED live on the real UDHR pipeline: letter-SET Jaccard distance (1 - |A∩B|/|A∪B| over unique characters, zero frequencies, zero n-grams, zero entropy) scores RF=10/22=0.455, identical to the trigram-JS headline; unigram-JS scores 0.545 (worse). The recovered Latin-script tree is driven by which diacritic/letter inventory each language uses (Polish ł/ż, Czech ř/ů, Turkish ı/ş/ğ), not by deep statistical structure. Without this comparison the whole project — an information-theory course project — cannot claim trigrams or Shannon/JS machinery do any work, and an examiner can dismiss the entire result in one line.
new_gaps.fix: Add three baselines to the RF/cophenetic table: (1) alphabet-set Jaccard, (2) unigram-frequency JS, (3) within-language char-shuffle negative control (kills order, keeps inventory — measured ~0.71, still beats random 0.88, so disclose it). If trigrams don't beat the inventory baselines on the new corpus, reframe honestly ('orthographic inventory suffices for Latin-script families; higher-order n-grams add nothing here') OR move to a regime where they must help: same-script same-alphabet languages whose letter sets are near-identical so only frequencies/n-grams can separate them.
new_gaps.depends_on: Corpus swap to FLORES+ (different inventory profile may change whether the tie persists); compute alongside the new estimators/bias work.

new_gaps.title: Turkish casefold bug: clean() turns the combining dot from İ.casefold() into a SPACE, injecting fake word breaks and corrupting Turkish trigrams in every tree/purity/RF
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED: 'İSTANBUL İNSAN'.casefold() = 'i'+U+0307(Mn)+'stanbul ...'; langtree.clean()'s keep-only-category-'L' comprehension (langtree.py:36) maps the U+0307 combining mark to ' ', yielding 'i stanbul i nsan'. The default path is keep_diacritics=True, so the NFKD/strip-combining branch never runs and the mark survives to the filter. Turkish UDHR has 7 'İ', each becomes a spurious boundary, injecting bogus '_st','i_' trigrams. This is a wrong-result code bug (not missing validation), and Turkish is the single Turkic data point in Tier 1 AND Tier 2 — exactly the language whose placement is already shaky (it lands as sister to all Germanic+Romance). Distinct from known #9 (mojibake/control chars): the text decodes fine, the cleaner mis-cleans it.
new_gaps.fix: In clean(), map Unicode category 'M' (combining marks) to '' not ' ' (delete, don't space-substitute), or NFC-normalize after casefold. Add a unit test asserting clean('İstanbul')=='istanbul'. Re-run Tier 1/2 and confirm Turkish trigrams change. One-line fix; do it before any corpus swap so the new pipeline inherits the corrected cleaner.
new_gaps.depends_on: None — fix immediately in langtree.clean(); precedes corpus swap and all estimator work since both reuse clean().

new_gaps.title: Normalized-RF denominator 2(n-3)=22 is wrong AND a topologically-perfect tree cannot score 0, because the gold tree is multifurcating — every headline RF is on a false scale
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED with dendropy on the actual GOLD string: gold has only 9 non-trivial bipartitions (basal IE|Uralic|Turkic trifurcation + German/Dutch/English trichotomy); any scipy-linkage inferred tree is strictly binary (10-11 bipartitions). A binary tree perfectly CONSISTENT with gold still scores RF>0 (never 0), and 2(n-3)=22 overstates the true max symmetric difference (|A|+|B|≈19-20). So 'normalized so 0=perfect' (notebook/langtree.py:158) is unattainable by construction and the quoted 0.45/0.64 are deflated relative to the honest scale (≈0.50/0.70). The JS<NCD<random ordering survives, but the absolute 'how close to truth' numbers the writeup leads with are not meaningful fractions. This is the most-flagged issue (5 of 6 lenses) and is the right thing to fix once, centrally. Note it overlaps known #4 (reliability) only loosely — this is a metric-correctness bug, not a missing bootstrap.
new_gaps.fix: Normalize by the sum of non-trivial bipartitions in the two specific trees (denom=|A|+|B|), OR report raw RF counts plus the achievable floor and the empirical max-over-shuffles (20) as denominator. Best combined fix: collapse unsupported inferred branches (via cophenetic/bootstrap, see reliability gap) before scoring so refinement of gold's polytomies isn't penalized; also make gold fully bifurcating with sourced deep splits if an ultrametric-comparable [0,1] scale is wanted. Drop the '0=perfect' claim and state the rooted-vs-unrooted treatment explicitly.
new_gaps.depends_on: Should land with the NJ/tree-methods and reliability work (branch collapsing needs bootstrap support); requires the gold-tree-sourcing decision (open question).

new_gaps.title: Cophenetic correlation (0.97 JS / 0.98 NCD) is never reported — the project leads with RF, the metric that looks worst, and omits the standard tree-quality diagnostic
new_gaps.why_it_matters: On the real pipeline the UPGMA cophenetic correlation is ~0.973 (JS) and ~0.984 (NCD): the dendrogram represents the actual distance matrix almost perfectly, yet RF is a mediocre 0.45/0.64. This divergence shows the trees faithfully encode the measured distances and the 'error' RF reports is mostly that the DISTANCES don't perfectly match genealogy, not that the tree-builder failed. RF is also coarse/all-or-nothing: one mis-rooted deep branch flips many bipartitions. A skeptical examiner will note the project quotes only the worst-looking metric and omits the diagnostic every phylogenetics paper reports. This is on the known list as part of #4 only as a plan; the specific point that the project leads with the wrong metric and the honest cophenetic number is high is new.
new_gaps.fix: Add and report cophenetic correlation (scipy.cluster.hierarchy.cophenet) for both JS and NCD, plus at least one finer topological metric (quartet distance or RF normalized against the random baseline). Frame RF as one of several diagnostics, not the verdict.
new_gaps.depends_on: Tree-methods stage (compute alongside NJ); cheap, low effort.

new_gaps.title: The random null permutes labels on ONE fixed topology and can never reach low RF (min 0.636) — it is the wrong null and overstates the gap to chance
new_gaps.why_it_matters: tier1_gold.py:27-33 holds the inferred JS dendrogram's SHAPE fixed and only shuffles the 14 leaf labels, then reports the MEAN (~0.88) as 'chance'. The null takes only 4 discrete values {0.64,0.73,0.82,0.91} and its MINIMUM is 0.636 — it literally cannot explore the low-RF region, so reporting 0.88 as the chance level inflates the apparent gap. It also answers the wrong question ('are labels in meaningful positions on THIS shape') instead of 'is this tree shape better than a random tree'; the baseline mean even depends on the fixed shape used (caterpillar 0.90 vs balanced 0.87). It is computed only for JS then implicitly applied to NCD. Distinct from known #4 (bootstrap branch support is about the tree's own stability; this is about the comparison null).
new_gaps.fix: Replace with a null over random tree TOPOLOGIES: draw N random binary trees on the 14 taxa (dendropy uniform/Yule simulators, or random join orders), score each vs GOLD, and report the observed RF as an empirical percentile/p-value against that distribution (min, 5th/50th/95th percentiles, not just the mean). Compute the baseline separately for the NCD topology.
new_gaps.depends_on: Tree-methods + reliability stage; uses the same corrected RF denominator, so do it after the RF-scale fix.

new_gaps.title: No confidence intervals or significance tests on ANY scalar (distances, r, RF, purity); leave-one-out shows the headline RF swings 0.25–0.60
new_gaps.why_it_matters: Every headline number is a single point estimate. Leave-one-language-out makes JS normalized RF range 0.25 (drop English) to 0.60 (drop Spanish/Portuguese), mean ~0.47, std ~0.09 — so 'JS (0.45) beats NCD (0.64)' rests on one draw from a wide distribution and a 1-bipartition change flips the number by ~0.09. The JS-vs-NCD correlation r is computed over C(14,2)=91 NON-INDEPENDENT pairwise distances (each language in 13 pairs), so the naive Pearson p-value is invalid. Tier-2 purity moves in steps of 1/9≈0.11 with no test. Distinct from known #4, which is about tree branch support, not uncertainty on the summary scalars the conclusions quote.
new_gaps.fix: Bootstrap over n-gram tokens (resample the trigram multiset per language) and over the language sample to put CIs on distances, RF, and purity; report the headline as e.g. 0.47±0.09 and only claim JS<NCD if a PAIRED bootstrap difference excludes 0. For the JS-NCD agreement use a Mantel permutation test, not a naive Pearson p. Treat purity as a small-sample proportion with a permutation/binomial test.
new_gaps.depends_on: Reliability stage; the token-bootstrap machinery is shared with branch support and JS-variance estimation, so build it once.

new_gaps.title: n=3 is the unique argmin of the n-gram order sweep (0.545/0.545/0.455/0.545/0.636) — looks like tuning the hyperparameter on the evaluation target
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED sweep against gold: n=1→0.545, n=2→0.545, n=3→0.455, n=4→0.545, n=5→0.636. Trigrams are the single global minimum and every other order is strictly worse; the project picks exactly n=3 and reports only that number. Known #10 notes the sweep is missing; the new and damaging point is that the chosen value is precisely the argmin on the same gold tree used to evaluate, so the headline is an optimistically selected point estimate, and the margin over n=1/2 is one bipartition (within noise).
new_gaps.fix: Either fix n a priori on a principled, pre-registered ground (e.g. the entropy-estimation wall / held-out perplexity) and report it before seeing RF, or report the full RF-vs-n curve and explicitly state n was not selected on the test tree. Combine with the bias-corrected entropy ladder so the order choice is justified by estimator behavior, not by RF.
new_gaps.depends_on: Estimator/bias stage (principled order selection uses held-out perplexity / entropy wall).

new_gaps.title: NLTK UDHR files are hard-capped at 10000 BYTES, so multibyte scripts cover only ~half the document — the 'same text in every language' control is false
new_gaps.why_it_matters: The NLTK udhr files are truncated at 10000 BYTES on disk (English-Latin1 is exactly 10000 bytes, ends mid-word). For 2-byte Cyrillic/Greek and Hebrew this yields far fewer characters and only ~the first half of the Declaration (measured: English 10000, Russian 5461, Greek 5471, Kazakh 5437, Hebrew 5613 decoded chars). The notebook's headline control claim — 'the same text in every language, so any difference is between languages not topics' — is therefore false for every cross-script pair: part of each distance is a difference in WHICH articles are included. Distinct from known #1 (corpus size/genre): this is a content-MISALIGNMENT artifact. Note this is largely MOOTED by the corpus swap (FLORES is line-aligned), so its main value is to confirm the swap is necessary and to fix any residual UDHR sanity-check arm.
new_gaps.fix: Stop trusting NLTK's truncated UDHR. The fix is the planned corpus swap to FLORES+ (line-aligned, same sentences). If UDHR is kept as a sanity-check control, align by article range (not byte/char count) and report decoded-char counts per language; stop claiming content-alignment until then.
new_gaps.depends_on: Subsumed by corpus swap (do that first); only a residual concern if UDHR is retained as a control arm.

new_gaps.title: equalize() truncates every text to the shortest member, making distances a property of the POOL (and the corrupt outlier) rather than of a language pair
new_gaps.why_it_matters: tier1.py:35-36 and tier2.py:31-33 cut every text to min(len) and keep a contiguous PREFIX. Three problems: (1) the common length depends on WHICH languages are pooled, so dropping the shortest language shifts ALL distances and the whole topology (English-German JS moved 0.760→0.763 purely from a pool change) — distances are not pair-invariant; (2) in Tier 2 the minimum (~5268 chars) is set by the CORRUPT Kazakh mojibake file, so ~4400 chars of clean Latin data are discarded to match garbage, and the prefix cut covers different article ranges per language (content confound); (3) the comment claims it 'controls finite-sample bias' but equalizing length does not remove plug-in bias (bias depends on K and N, which differ by language). Overlaps known #8 (RAW vs ROMANIZED unequal budgets) but the within-arm pool-dependence and the corrupt-file-sets-the-budget issues are new.
new_gaps.fix: Use full text (estimators normalize to probabilities anyway), or if equalization is wanted for fairness, subsample tokens with replacement to a FIXED budget and average over several draws (removes pool-dependence and prefix bias). Exclude corrupt files from the budget. Use the same budget for RAW and ROMANIZED arms and report the retained fraction per language. Correct the misleading 'controls finite-sample bias' comment.
new_gaps.depends_on: Corpus swap (FLORES gives equal sentence counts, partly mooting this) + estimator/bias stage; align with known #8.

new_gaps.title: gzip NCD violates the metric axioms it relies on: self-distance ≈0.034 (not 0), C(xy)≠C(yx), and UTF-8 byte budget biases multi-byte-accent languages
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED: NCD(x,x)≈0.034 (gzip doesn't dedupe a doubled 16KB text even though it fits the 32KB window — dictionary inefficiency, not window overflow), so the diagonal is force-zeroed by np.fill_diagonal but off-diagonal close pairs carry a ~0.034 incompressibility floor; C(xy)≠C(yx) by ~8 bytes yet the code uses only raw[i]+raw[j] (arbitrary undocumented order); and because clean() keeps diacritics and texts are UTF-8, languages with more multi-byte accented chars get longer byte strings at equal character length, biasing C(x) and hence NCD by orthography independent of content. These pathologies undermine NCD as the 'independent cross-check' that the r=0.87 agreement and NCD RF=0.64 lean on, and partly explain why NCD scores worse than JS.
new_gaps.fix: Symmetrize (use min or avg of C(xy),C(yx)); subtract/report the self-NCD floor; insert a rare separator byte at the seam; encode consistently or transliterate to single-byte so byte budget tracks character budget; subtract the 20-byte empty-stream overhead. State gzip-NCD is a crude Kolmogorov proxy and consider bz2/zstd-with-dictionary, or report NCD only qualitatively.
new_gaps.depends_on: Estimator/bias stage (NCD is one of the two estimators); independent of the JS bias-correction work but do them together.

new_gaps.title: Tier-2 raw cross-script JS is exactly 1.000 for ALL 29 cross-script pairs, so the raw topology and nearest-neighbours are arbitrary tie-breaks, and the 0.44→1.00 purity jump partly just removes this degeneracy
new_gaps.why_it_matters: On raw mixed-script text, languages in different scripts have disjoint trigram vocabularies, so JS=1.000 for every cross-script pair (min=max=mean=1.000). Consequence: Greek, Hebrew, AND Arabic all report nearest-neighbour=English purely because every candidate ties at 1.000 and Python's stable sort returns the first dict element — change the dict order and these assignments change. The raw dendrogram's cross-script branching is UPGMA tie-breaking on a wall of identical 1.000s, not a finding, so the 'raw clusters by script' narrative is only half-supported and 'raw purity 0.44' is partly a structural artifact. Distinct from known #5 (no Tier-2 RF): this is about the raw arm being degenerate, not the romanized arm lacking a score.
new_gaps.fix: Detect and flag tied distances; explicitly report that raw cross-script NN is undefined and do not present the raw cross-script topology as a result. Quantify the romanized arm with an RF score against a Tier-2 gold tree (known #5) and report how many NN ties were broken arbitrarily.
new_gaps.depends_on: Tree-methods/claims stage; needs the Tier-2 gold tree (ties to known #5).

new_gaps.title: Tier-2 family-purity has a ~0.33 chance floor (5 of 9 languages lumped as one 'Indo-European') and singleton scripts cap script-purity at 0.67 — the 0.44→1.00 jump is not interpreted against either
new_gaps.why_it_matters: tier2.py labels English/Spanish/German/Russian/Greek all as one family 'Indo-European', so random-NN family-purity ≈0.33 and the raw 0.44 is barely above chance; the coarse bucket also lets Russian↔Greek or German↔English count as family 'hits' across genealogically deep IE branches. Separately, 3 scripts are singletons (Greek/Hebrew/Arabic) that can NEVER match by script, structurally capping script-purity at 6/9≈0.67 — which is exactly the reported raw value, so the 'confounded by script' number is partly a ceiling, not evidence. With n=9 and ±0.11 steps, '0.44→1.00' is 5 NN flips with no significance test, and 'Semitic recovered'/'Turkic recovered' are each a single 2-language pair. Linked to known #5 but the chance-baseline and singleton-ceiling biases of this specific metric are new.
new_gaps.fix: Use branch-level family labels (Germanic/Romance/Slavic/Hellenic/Turkic/Semitic) so IE isn't one giant bucket; report purity against a permutation/random-NN baseline (~0.33) and acknowledge the singleton-script ceiling; add the Tier-2 RF score against a gold tree. With the new corpus, add multiple members per family so the denominator is meaningful.
new_gaps.depends_on: Corpus swap (more members per family) + claims/controls stage; ties to known #5.

new_gaps.title: The 5-cluster report() that 'validates family recovery' lumps all 9 Germanic+Romance languages into one MIXED cluster, and scores singleton clusters as OK — directly contradicting the prose
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED logic: tier1.py:56-64 reports fcluster(Z,t=5,maxclust) and flags 'OK' iff a cluster has one family. The JS 5-cluster output is {English,German,Dutch,Swedish,Danish,Spanish,Italian,French,Portuguese} = one MIXED cluster (Germanic and Romance NOT separated), while Finnish and Hungarian land in SEPARATE singleton clusters flagged 'OK' only because a one-member cluster trivially has one family. So 'Uralic (Finnish-Hungarian) separates out' is FALSE (they never group; their actual NNs are Swedish/Turkish), 'Romance forms a clean clade' holds only at finer cuts (English nests inside Romance), and the script's own quantitative check contradicts the README/notebook claims. This is a code-logic + claims bug, sharper than known #6 (which only covers the borrowing/Semitic claims).
new_gaps.fix: Change the flag so singleton clusters are NOT scored as successes (require ≥2 members, or report adjusted Rand index / homogeneity+completeness vs family labels). Correct the prose: Uralic is not recovered, Germanic/Romance do not separate at k=5, English sits with Romance, and (after the casefold fix) report where Turkish actually lands. Report the k at which each family first becomes monophyletic (Germanic/Romance never do on this data).
new_gaps.depends_on: Casefold fix (changes Turkish position) + claims/interpretation stage; cheap once the tree is final.

new_gaps.title: English↔Romance is a Latinate-orthography + legal-register artifact (-tion/-ion/-ent), not 'Norman borrowing'; and the mechanism is traceable
new_gaps.why_it_matters: The English-French pull is dominated by trigrams 'tio'(EN84/FR79/DE14), 'ion'(EN98/FR94/DE19), 'ent'(EN49/FR66/DE16) — shared Latinate SPELLING conventions, amplified by the UDHR's formal-legal register where ~3.6% of English word tokens end in -tion (declaration, education, discrimination...) vs ~0.3-0.5% in ordinary prose, an ~8-10x inflation. A character-trigram method on Latin-orthography text cannot distinguish genuine borrowing from shared spelling conventions from register-specific word choice; 'Norman borrowing' (11th-15th c.) is also the wrong mechanism since most -tion words are later learned Latin borrowings. Known #6 lists the borrowing claim as overstated; the new value is the traced mechanism and the testable fix.
new_gaps.fix: Retitle to 'shared Latinate orthography/register'. Demonstrate it: show the top responsible trigrams and the -tion token rate; re-run after stripping the -tion/-ion/-ent suffix family and show the English-Romance pull collapses. State trigrams cannot localize the borrowing layer. Re-test on the non-legal FLORES corpus — if the pull weakens, that confirms register.
new_gaps.depends_on: Corpus swap (FLORES non-legal register is the cleanest test) + claims/interpretation stage.

new_gaps.title: 'Semitic recovered' is likely a vowelless-abjad + non-neutral-uroman artifact, and the success claim self-contradicts its own disclaimer
new_gaps.why_it_matters: Hebrew-Arabic becoming mutual NN after uroman is framed as 'Semitic re-emerges', but uroman renders both abjads as VOWELLESS consonant skeletons (neither writes short vowels) while it gives Greek/Russian full vowels — an asymmetric, lossy transform across the very pair being compared, which can MANUFACTURE similarity (shared property of missing-vowels + small shared consonant inventory) rather than reveal genealogy. The notebook itself admits 'vowelless consonant skeletons' two sentences after claiming recovery — the claim and its disclaimer contradict. With one Semitic pair, 'mutual NN' is a 2-point test, not family recovery. Extends known #6/#7 with a specific success-framing failure.
new_gaps.fix: Downgrade to: 'Hebrew and Arabic become mutual NN after romanization, but this likely reflects shared abjad structure plus a non-neutral uroman mapping, not recovered genealogy.' Add a control: romanize with vowel restoration for one language, or compare against a non-Semitic abjad-rendered language. Add more Semitic/abjad members (the new corpus has Arabic+Hebrew but consider adding another) so it isn't a 2-point coincidence.
new_gaps.depends_on: Claims/interpretation + controls stage; ties to known #7 (uroman neutrality).

new_gaps.title: Orthographic-depth confound is uncontrolled: deep-orthography English vs shallow Finnish/Turkish makes trigram distance partly a spelling-transparency measure
new_gaps.why_it_matters: Character trigrams over native orthography conflate three things the project treats as one ('language similarity'): genealogy, phonology, and orthographic depth. English has opaque deep orthography (ght, silent letters, -tion); Finnish/Turkish are near-phonemic. So two shallow-orthography languages read as similar partly because both spell-as-they-sound, and English reads Romance-like partly via Latinate digraphs — independent of genealogy or phonology. The README mentions 'orthographic/surface similarity' generically but never names orthographic depth, and there is no control. This means the central question ('does similarity track genealogy, orthography, or phonology?') is currently answered 'orthography, mostly' and the three are not isolated. Compounds the alphabet-baseline finding.
new_gaps.fix: Add a phonemic/IPA arm (e.g. epitran) for the Latin-script set and compare trees: if genealogy survives transcription the signal is phonological/lexical; if it changes it was orthographic. At minimum, list orthographic depth as a named confound and flag English's deep orthography as a likely driver of its Romance placement. (High effort; can be a stretch goal.)
new_gaps.depends_on: Corpus swap + claims/controls stage; highest-effort item, schedule last among controls.

new_gaps.title: UDHR shared boilerplate inflates similarity template-wide, and a one-line header strip does NOT fix it
new_gaps.why_it_matters: Every translation repeats a templated structure (title, 'Preamble', 'Whereas...', 'Article 1..30', recurring legalese + internationalisms like declaration/dignity/universal). clean() strips only non-letters, so this shared scaffolding and shared loanwords survive and recur near-identically across Romance/Germanic, so measured similarity is partly similarity of a SHARED TEMPLATE, not intrinsic language statistics — a confound that flatters the result. Stripping the first 300-1500 chars (title+preamble) barely moves NN-purity or English-French distance, proving the effect is diffuse and template-wide, so the known #11 'strip title/header lines' fix is insufficient. Largely mooted by the corpus swap but worth a residual control.
new_gaps.fix: Strip structural markers ('Preamble','Article',numbers) before n-gramming, AND — the real fix — demonstrate the families still recover on the non-parallel multi-genre FLORES/Leipzig corpora. Add a control measuring boilerplate-only vs body-only distances to quantify the template's contribution. Distinct from known #11 because the diffuse template-wide nature requires a different remedy than header-stripping.
new_gaps.depends_on: Corpus swap (the strongest rebuttal) + controls stage; extends known #11.

new_gaps.title: Gold tree is a hand-typed, partly-debatable topology used as 'ground truth' with no Glottolog citation, and its choices directly move the RF headline
new_gaps.why_it_matters: GOLD (tier1_gold.py:12-14) is asserted as 'the true Glottolog family tree' but is hand-built with contestable calls: the Romance split ((Spanish,Portuguese),(Italian,French)) contradicts standard Western-Romance subgrouping (French groups with Ibero-Romance, Italian is the outlier); English is a flat sister of German/Dutch (it's Anglo-Frisian, not flat West-Germanic); the root is a trifurcation designed rooted but scored unrooted. Since the single RF number is the project's only quantitative success claim, its dependence on a non-authoritative hand tree (and the circularity risk that the method designers also drew the evaluation target) is a validity threat. Glottolog is a catalogue, not a dated bifurcating tree, so no node is sourced.
new_gaps.fix: Derive the gold topology programmatically from Glottolog / lang2vec genetic distances and document the source; ensure rooted-vs-unrooted treatment matches between gold and inferred; run RF sensitivity under 2-3 plausible gold topologies to show 0.45 is robust (or not).
new_gaps.depends_on: This is an OPEN QUESTION fork (hand-fix vs lang2vec-derive); resolve before the RF-scale and reliability work since the denominator and floor depend on the gold's resolution.

new_gaps.title: No version pinning / requirements file; README local-install omits dendropy and uroman is not actually importable — tier1_gold.py and tier2.py are not reproducible as documented
new_gaps.why_it_matters: VERIFIED: README says 'pip install nltk uroman scipy matplotlib' but tier1_gold.py imports dendropy (unlisted), so following the README verbatim crashes the gold/RF step; there is no requirements.txt/lockfile and nothing is pinned. uroman in particular is under active development (its transliteration tables and even Perl-vs-Python packaging differ), so every Tier-2 romanized distance and the purity 1.00 depend on whatever version ships on the day; the NLTK udhr corpus has also changed historically. A grader cannot reproduce the exact numbers.
new_gaps.fix: Add a pinned requirements.txt (nltk==, scipy==, numpy==, dendropy==, uroman==, matplotlib== and now datasets/huggingface for FLORES) and pin the same in the notebook pip cell; list dendropy in README; print all library versions and per-language decoded-char counts at the top of each run; record the corpus version/hash.
new_gaps.depends_on: Do during notebook regen + README stage (last), after the corpus and code are final.

false_alarms.claim: Union-vocab construction makes a fixed language pair's JS depend on which other languages are pooled (a concern raised then dismissed within the stats lens itself).
false_alarms.why_not_real: Correctly dismissed and I confirmed it in code: absent trigrams are 0 in BOTH probability vectors (ngram_dist fills missing keys with 0/tot), and jensenshannon over zero-padded dimensions is unaffected by extra all-zero coordinates. The pairwise JS of a fixed pair does NOT change when other languages are added to the vocab. The real pool-dependence is via equalize() truncation length (captured as a separate, genuine gap), not via the vocabulary. Do not spend effort 'fixing' the union vocab.

false_alarms.claim: gzip NCD is non-deterministic run-to-run because gzip embeds an mtime, and/or the self-distance floor is caused by the 32KB window being overflowed by the concatenation.
false_alarms.why_not_real: VERIFIED both are false: gzip.compress length is deterministic across runs (the embedded mtime does not change the byte length), so there is no run-to-run nondeterminism to fix. And the concatenated UDHR texts (~16KB) fit comfortably inside gzip's 32KB window, so the ~0.034 self-NCD floor is dictionary/back-reference inefficiency, NOT window overflow — adding a larger window or chunking won't remove it. The real NCD issues (self-floor, asymmetry, UTF-8 byte bias) are captured in the genuine NCD gap; don't chase a phantom window-overflow or mtime bug.

false_alarms.claim: The Shannon F1 'validation' (plug-in F1≈4.0 matching Shannon 4.03) is a NEW logical flaw to flag.
false_alarms.why_not_real: The underlying point (F1 is essentially bias-free at K=27/N≈9657 so it validates nothing about the biased n=3 estimator the method actually uses) is correct and worth fixing, but the lens itself marked it is_new=false because it restates known gap #2 (bias correction). Folding it into the bias-correction work (report Miller-Madow/Grassberger-corrected F2/F3 with estimated bias) covers it. Don't open a separate workstream; just stop presenting F1's match as validation of the trigram method.

false_alarms.claim: Kazakh mojibake and Arabic stray control characters are new data bugs to add to the plan.
false_alarms.why_not_real: Both lenses explicitly marked these is_new=false — they are known gap #9. The new knock-on detail (Kazakh corruption setting the Tier-2 truncation budget) is already folded into the genuine 'equalize() pool-dependence' gap. More importantly, the corpus swap to FLORES (Kazakh = kaz_Cyrl, clean, already on disk) and the per-language Unicode-block sanity check (known #9's planned fix) resolve these; no separate action needed beyond executing #9 + the swap.

false_alarms.claim: The redundant np.fill_diagonal(D,0.0) in ncd_matrix is a bug worth fixing.
false_alarms.why_not_real: It is cosmetic, not a correctness issue: D is initialized to zeros and only the i<j triangle is written, so the diagonal is already 0 and fill_diagonal is a harmless no-op. The real NCD concern is that it MASKS the ~0.034 self-distance floor (captured in the NCD gap). Removing the redundant line is fine housekeeping but should not be tracked as a real gap or consume review effort on its own.

corpus_recommendation.primary: FLORES+ (HuggingFace openlanguagedata/flores_plus), or equivalently FLORES-200 (facebook/flores) via plain tarball to skip HF gating. It is the ONLY n-way content-controlled corpus covering all four special languages INCLUDING KAZAKH (tur_Latn, heb_Hebr=Modern Hebrew, arb_Arab=MSA, kaz_Cyrl) PLUS the full 16-language European anchor set — the same ~2009 sentences (dev 997 + devtest 1012) translated into every language, CC-BY-SA-4.0, one-line per-language load joined by id+split. Same-content is exactly what an information-theoretic family tree needs (differences are linguistic, not topical), and the encyclopedic/news register is a genuine departure from UDHR legalese (directly fixing the boilerplate/single-genre gaps). IMPORTANT CONTEXT: the corpus work is already underway — corpus/flores/aligned/ already contains line-aligned he/kk/tr/ar (2009 lines each, including Kazakh), so the FLORES path is proven for the specials; the remaining task is to pull the 16 European codes (eng/spa/por/ita/fra/deu/nld/swe/dan/pol/ces/rus/ell/fin/hun) into the same aligned set.
corpus_recommendation.secondary: CC-100 (statmt/cc100) for the volume + genre-replication arm: covers all 19 including Kazakh (kk, 889M chars), ungated one-line load_dataset('statmt/cc100', lang='kk'), web genre distinct from both UDHR and FLORES+. Use it to show the family tree REPLICATES when swapping from controlled-parallel encyclopedic text to large monolingual web text — a strong robustness argument. NOTE: Leipzig mono (he/kk/tr/ar 300K sentences each) is ALREADY on disk in corpus/mono/, so Leipzig is the path-of-least-resistance secondary for the 4 specials (cite Goldhahn 2012, CC-BY-NC, non-commercial); use CC-100 or FineWeb-2 (ODC-By, cleaner license, Kazakh ~2B words) if a clearly-open license or the full 19-language monolingual set is wanted.
corpus_recommendation.languages_resolved: ALL 19 targets including KAZAKH are covered by the recommended stack. Kazakh — the binding constraint that disqualifies most candidates — is SOLVED: present in FLORES+ as kaz_Cyrl (content-controlled, already downloaded and line-aligned on disk) and in CC-100/FineWeb-2/Leipzig/mC4 for volume. Modern Hebrew is correctly heb_Hebr in FLORES (a Bible-based corpus would wrongly give Biblical/Masoretic Hebrew — see risks). The 4 specials (kk/he/tr/ar) are already built; the 16 European anchors still need to be extracted into the aligned FLORES set.
corpus_recommendation.risks: (1) FLORES+ is small (~2009 sentences/lang) — fine for a content-controlled DISTANCE matrix (the tree backbone) but thin for high-order (n=3) entropy estimation; pair with the monolingual secondary for entropy/volume and report n-gram order sensitivity. (2) FLORES+ is HF-gated (accept terms once); use the FLORES-200 tarball if gating is a problem. (3) Leipzig is CC-BY-NC (non-commercial academic only) and sentence-order-randomized (no document context — fine for n-gram entropy, bad for anything needing context); FineWeb-2 (ODC-By) avoids the license restriction. (4) FLORES is professional translation, so mild translationese is present in all languages equally (acceptable for a controlled comparison). (5) The Bible-corpus route was investigated and REJECTED: christos-c, OPUS bible-uedin, BibleNLP/eBible, and open-bibles ALL cover the other 18 but ALL LACK KAZAKH (verified against file lists and the OPUS API; a web snippet claiming bible-uedin has 'kk' is wrong), and the only Kazakh Bibles (Kyeli Kitap 2010, KBS 2015, Bible Mission 2023, JW NWT) are copyrighted online/app-only with no redistributable bulk download and no shared alignment — do NOT scrape kkitap.net or extract JW EPUBs (proprietary). Bible 'Hebrew' is also Masoretic, the wrong register for a modern-language tree.
corpus_recommendation.fallback: If FLORES HF access or the European-anchor extraction stalls, fall back to CC-100 (ungated, all 19 incl. Kazakh, one-line load) as a MONOLINGUAL backbone — accepting it does not control content, so report that caveat. If you want to demonstrate genre robustness beyond two corpora, add TED2020 (spoken) or OpenSubtitles (informal dialogue) as tertiary supplements (both cover all 19 incl. Kazakh, but pairwise English-pivot and small Kazakh, so use only as supplements, not the controlled backbone). AVOID entirely for the full set: wiki40b, MASSIVE (no Kazakh), Tanzil (no Hebrew/Kazakh + scripture), MultiUN, Europarl (missing several specials), and Tatoeba/CCMatrix/WikiMatrix (not content-controlled; CCMatrix/WikiMatrix have no Kazakh-Hebrew bitext at all). Keep UDHR only as a small same-document sanity-check control, not the main corpus.

execution_order: STAGE 0 — Quick code bugfixes that everything else inherits (do FIRST, independent of corpus): (a) Fix the Turkish casefold bug in langtree.clean() — map combining marks (category 'M') to '' not ' ', add unit test clean('İstanbul')=='istanbul' [NEW critical]. (b) Fix the report() flag so singleton clusters are not scored 'OK' [NEW major]. (c) Remove duplicated gold-tree helper code [known #11]. These reuse-by-everything fixes must precede the corpus swap so the new pipeline inherits a correct cleaner.

STAGE 1 — Corpus foundation: Swap to FLORES+ as primary (pull the 16 European anchors into corpus/flores/aligned/ to join the already-built he/kk/tr/ar incl. Kazakh) + wire CC-100/Leipzig as the monolingual secondary; this simultaneously resolves known #1 (tiny single-genre corpus), the NLTK 10000-byte truncation [NEW major], much of the equalize()/pool-dependence problem [NEW major], and the UDHR boilerplate confound [NEW + known #11]. Add per-language Unicode-block + decoded-char sanity checks [known #9], which catches any residual Kazakh/Arabic decode issues. Equalize by aligned sentence count, equal budget across RAW/ROMANIZED arms [known #8].

STAGE 2 — Estimators & bias correction (on the new corpus): Add add-k/Dirichlet smoothing + Grassberger/Miller-Madow entropy estimator and report estimated bias on F2/F3 [known #2], which also subsumes the Shannon-F1-validation false-validation point; add JS smoothing to combat saturation [NEW major]; symmetrize gzip NCD, subtract the self-floor, add a seam separator, fix the UTF-8 byte-budget bias [NEW major]; run the n-gram order sweep and select n by a PRINCIPLED criterion (held-out perplexity / entropy wall) rather than RF-argmin [known #10 + NEW n=argmin major].

STAGE 3 — Tree methods: Add Neighbor-Joining (unrooted, no molecular-clock assumption) and compare to UPGMA [known #3]; report both rooted and unrooted RF so UPGMA's deep-rooting errors (Slavic/Turkic/Uralic misplacement) are visible; add the alphabet-set-Jaccard, unigram-JS, and within-language char-shuffle baselines to the RF table [NEW critical — the single highest-value fix]; add the Tier-2 gold tree + RF score and flag the raw cross-script JS=1.000 degeneracy [known #5 + NEW major].

STAGE 4 — Reliability & metric correctness: Fix the RF normalization (denom = sum of non-trivial bipartitions of the two specific trees; drop '0=perfect'; collapse unsupported branches before scoring) [NEW major, most-flagged]; replace the label-permutation null with a random-tree-topology null reporting an empirical p-value [NEW major]; add cophenetic correlation (~0.97/0.98) as a primary diagnostic [NEW major]; add token-and-sample bootstrap CIs on distances/RF/purity and a paired JS-vs-NCD test + a Mantel test for the JS-NCD r [NEW major]; add bootstrap branch support and collapse low-support deep splits [known #4].

STAGE 5 — Claims, interpretation & controls: Retitle 'Norman borrowing' → 'Latinate orthography/register' and show the -tion/-ion/-ent mechanism + suffix-stripping collapse test [NEW major]; downgrade 'Semitic recovered' to the abjad/uroman-artifact framing + vowel-restoration control [NEW major, known #6/#7]; correct the Tier-1 prose (Uralic NOT recovered, Germanic/Romance not separated at k=5, English nests in Romance, Turkish misplaced) [NEW major]; fix the Tier-2 purity chance-baseline and singleton-script ceiling with branch-level labels + permutation p-value [NEW major, known #5]; add the orthographic-depth confound discussion and (stretch) an IPA/epitran arm [NEW major, highest effort — last]; assess uroman neutrality [known #7].

STAGE 6 — Reproducibility, notebook regen & README: Resolve/derive the gold tree (lang2vec/Glottolog vs documented hand-tree — see open question) and re-run RF sensitivity [NEW minor + known on gold]; add pinned requirements.txt incl. dendropy + datasets/huggingface, pin the notebook pip cell, list dendropy in README, print library versions + decoded-char counts [NEW minor]; regenerate the notebook from the corrected pipeline; add figure captions/axis-label meaning [known #12]; final README rewrite to match the honest results.
open_questions: GOLD TREE SOURCING (blocks the RF-scale and reliability work): derive the gold topology programmatically from Glottolog/lang2vec genetic distances (removes the hand-built circularity + sourcing objection, but lang2vec trees may be flat/uninformative at the family level), OR keep a documented hand-tree but make it fully bifurcating with sourced deep splits and run RF sensitivity over 2-3 plausible topologies? This determines the RF denominator, the achievable floor, and whether the headline number is defensible.

SCOPE OF THE LANGUAGE PANEL: keep the existing 14 (Tier-1) / 9 (Tier-2) panel on the new corpus, or expand to the full 19-language target set (the corpus README and FLORES both support all 19 incl. Kazakh)? More members per family is the real fix for the n=2-per-family purity and small-sample-CI problems, but it enlarges scope and changes every figure. Kazakh in Tier 1 also requires deciding its script handling (kaz_Cyrl raw vs romanized).

HOW HARD TO PUSH THE 'INFORMATION THEORY ADDS NOTHING' RESULT: given the verified alphabet-Jaccard tie (RF 0.455=0.455) and char-shuffle control (~0.71), if trigrams still don't beat the inventory baseline on FLORES, do you want to (a) honestly reframe the contribution as 'orthographic inventory suffices; n-grams add nothing here', or (b) pivot the experimental regime to same-script/same-alphabet languages where only frequencies can separate them so the information theory is forced to do work? This is a framing/scope fork that shapes the whole writeup.

HEBREW REGISTER / CORPUS ARM: FLORES gives Modern Hebrew (heb_Hebr) which is correct for a modern-language tree — confirm you do NOT want Biblical Hebrew (the rejected Bible corpora would have given Masoretic), and confirm Leipzig CC-BY-NC (non-commercial) is acceptable for the secondary or whether to switch to FineWeb-2 (ODC-By) for a cleaner license.
